title: Introduction to Rust Programming
description: Master Rust fundamentals through ownership, references, and pattern matching with adaptive branching and practice.

nodes.title: Rust Ownership Basics
nodes.content: Rust's ownership system ensures memory safety without garbage collection. Every value in Rust has a single owner responsible for deallocating it. When the owner goes out of scope, Rust automatically frees the memory. This eliminates null pointer exceptions and data races at compile time. Ownership is the foundation of Rust's safety guarantees.

nodes.title: Ownership in Action
nodes.content: When you write `let s1 = String::from("hello");` and then `let s2 = s1;`, ownership moves from s1 to s2—s1 becomes invalid. If you need to keep both variables, use a reference: `let s2 = &s1;` borrows s1 without taking ownership. This move semantics prevents use-after-free bugs while references enable sharing without losing ownership.

nodes.title: Ownership Checkpoint
nodes.content: Test your understanding of Rust's ownership and borrowing system.

questions.question: What happens when you assign one String to another variable?
options: Ownership moves; the original becomes invalid
feedback: Incorrect: Strings use move semantics, not copying
Correct! Ownership transfers in a move
Incorrect: Without explicit sharing, ownership is exclusive
Incorrect: Move semantics compile successfully
questions.explanation: Assignment transfers ownership (move) to prevent double-free. The original variable is no longer valid.

questions.question: How do you let multiple variables access the same value without moving ownership?
feedback: Incorrect: Cloning creates new owned copies
Correct! References borrow without transferring ownership
Incorrect: Copy doesn't enable sharing
Incorrect: Scopes don't enable multi-access
questions.explanation: References (&) allow borrowing data without taking ownership, letting multiple parts of code access the same value.

nodes.title: Ownership Reinforcement
nodes.content: Strengthen your understanding of ownership rules. Remember: moves transfer ownership, references borrow data, and mutable references allow changes. Each rule exists to prevent memory errors.

questions.question: Why does Rust restrict multiple mutable references to the same variable?
options: To prevent data races and corruption
To make code harder to write
feedback: Incorrect: Restriction is for safety, not performance
Correct! One mutable reference prevents concurrent modification
Incorrect: Restrictions improve safety
Incorrect: This is a safety rule, not style
questions.explanation: Only one mutable reference at a time prevents data races where multiple threads modify the same data.

questions.question: What error would this code produce? `let mut x = 5; let r1 = &mut x; let r2 = &mut x;`
options: Compiler error: cannot have two mutable references
No error; both references are valid
feedback: Incorrect: Rust catches this at compile time
Correct! The borrow checker prevents this compile-time
Incorrect: Rust forbids this
Incorrect: Rust prevents this, not permits it
questions.explanation: The borrow checker enforces that only one mutable reference exists at a time, catching this error during compilation.

nodes.title: Pattern Matching in Rust
nodes.content: Pattern matching is Rust's powerful way to destructure values and handle different cases. The `match` expression compares a value against multiple patterns and executes code for the first match. Patterns can match enums, structs, tuples, and values, extracting data in one operation. This makes complex logic concise while ensuring all cases are handled at compile time.

nodes.title: Pattern Matching Example
nodes.content: Consider: `match result { Ok(value) => println!("Success: {}", value), Err(e) => println!("Error: {}", e) }`. Each arm matches a pattern and binds variables you can use. With enums like `Option<T>`, patterns like `Some(x)` destructure and bind the inner value. Pattern matching forces you to handle all possibilities, eliminating unhandled error cases that plague other languages.

questions.question: What does the pattern `Some(n)` do in a match expression?
options: Matches Option::Some and binds the inner value to n
feedback: Incorrect: Some(n) does match Something
Correct! It destructures Some and binds the inner value
Incorrect: n is a binding pattern, not a value match
Incorrect: Some is the variant, n is the binding
questions.explanation: The pattern `Some(n)` matches the `Some` variant and destructures it, binding the contained value to variable `n`.

questions.question: What is the advantage of Rust's match expression over if-else statements?
options: Compiler ensures all cases are handled; patterns destructure data
feedback: Incorrect: Performance is similar
Correct! Match exhaustiveness and destructuring are powerful
Incorrect: Both can be readable
Incorrect: Both can return values
questions.explanation: Match enforces exhaustiveness (compiler error if cases are missed) and allows destructuring in one operation, preventing data access bugs.

nodes.title: Course Completion
nodes.content: Excellent progress! You've mastered Rust's ownership system and pattern matching—core concepts that make Rust unique and safe. These foundations enable you to write concurrent, memory-safe code without garbage collection.

nodes.title: Rust Fundamentals Mastered!
nodes.content: Congratulations! You've completed Introduction to Rust Programming. You now understand ownership (the core safety feature), borrowing through references, and pattern matching (destructuring with safety). These concepts work together to make Rust uniquely safe. Next, explore error handling with Result, lifetimes for complex references, and traits for abstraction. Build small projects to reinforce your learning. Welcome to the Rust community!
